issues.description: The Zeno 'walking toward a wall, only going halfway' analogy actually models a convergent infinite SUM / sequence (distances adding to a finite total), not the limit of a function f(x) as x approaches a value a. A learner may form the mental model that a limit means 'adding up infinitely many shrinking pieces,' which is a different concept (series) introduced much later, and may struggle to map this onto lim f(x) where nothing is being summed.
issues.fix: Re-anchor the analogy to a quantity approaching a value as an INPUT approaches a target: e.g. 'as the time on a clock creeps toward 3:00, what reading is the temperature heading toward' — input x approaching a, output f(x) approaching L. Or explicitly note the wall image shows the IDEA of getting arbitrarily close, and that here it's f(x) approaching L as x approaches a, not a running total.

issues.description: 'We're asking what f of x approaches as x sneaks up on a' is good, but pairing it with 'never has to actually be a' (also 05_notation) understates the truth. The limit is INDEPENDENT of f(a) entirely — f(a) can be undefined, or equal something completely unrelated, and the limit is unaffected. 'Never has to be a' leaves room for the misconception that f(a) still influences or partly determines the limit.
issues.fix: Add one sentence: 'The value f(a) is irrelevant to the limit — f could be undefined at a, or jump to a totally different value at exactly a, and the limit would not change at all. The limit only cares about the neighborhood around a, never a itself.'

issues.description: 'The function blows up to infinity, no single number it's heading toward' lumps the infinite case into plain DNE without the standard AP nuance. AP convention still writes lim = +infinity or -infinity as a meaningful description of the behavior (and distinguishes the two one-sided infinite limits, which can disagree, e.g. 1/x at 0). Stating it as undifferentiated 'DNE' can make a student think infinite behavior carries no further information and that they should never write +/- infinity, which the AP exam in fact rewards.
issues.fix: Clarify: 'The two-sided limit DNE as a finite number, but we still describe the behavior by writing lim = +infinity or -infinity, and the left and right sides can even head to opposite infinities (like 1/x at zero) — that's still DNE for the two-sided limit but the one-sided infinite limits carry real information.'

issues.description: 'Draw it through that point without lifting your pencil' is the classic pencil-test oversimplification. It is fine as intuition for continuity AT A POINT, but learners reliably over-generalize it to 'a continuous function can always be drawn without lifting your pencil anywhere,' which is false for functions continuous on their domain but with gaps/asymptotes outside it (e.g. 1/x is continuous everywhere on its domain yet cannot be drawn unbroken). The narration immediately gives the rigorous 3-condition test, which is good, but the pencil phrasing may stick harder than the formal definition.
issues.fix: Bracket the pencil image as point-local only: 'No lifting your pencil right AT that one point — but careful, that picture is just intuition for a single point; the real definition is the three conditions, and we always trust those over the pencil image.'

issues.description: The three discontinuity types are named correctly but the defining limit-behavior of each is not tied back to the one-sided / two-sided machinery just taught. A student is told to 'memorize those three names' without the discriminating rule: removable = two-sided limit EXISTS but does not equal (or f(a) undefined); jump = both one-sided limits exist but DIFFER; infinite = at least one one-sided limit is infinite. Memorizing names without the underlying test invites mis-classification (e.g. calling a jump 'removable' because there's a visible break).
issues.fix: Add one connecting line: 'The way to tell them apart is the limit itself — removable: the two-sided limit exists but f(a) doesn't match (or is missing); jump: the one-sided limits both exist but disagree; infinite: a one-sided limit runs off to infinity.'

issues.description: The pause/answer pair is internally consistent and correct (left=3, right=5, two-sided DNE). No mismatch. Noting only that the answer reveal could reinforce the trap by explicitly stating that the one-sided limits each still EXIST even though the two-sided DNE — it does say this, which is good. Flagging as a near-miss strength, not a defect.
issues.fix: Optional reinforcement: end with 'So the right full answer on the AP exam is: left-hand limit = 3, right-hand limit = 5, two-sided limit DNE — write all three, don't just write DNE.'

notes: No outright false statements, but several intuition-first phrasings (Zeno-as-limit, pencil test, undifferentiated infinite-DNE) risk durable wrong mental models unless tied back to the one-sided/two-sided definitions the lesson already teaches.
